flashcards.front: What is operator overloading for new and delete in C++?
flashcards.back: It allows customization of memory allocation and deallocation by defining custom versions of the global or class-specific new and delete operators to control how memory is managed.

flashcards.front: What is the syntax for overloading the global new operator?
flashcards.back: void* operator new(size_t size) { /* allocation logic */ return pointer; }

flashcards.front: What is the syntax for overloading the global delete operator?
flashcards.back: void operator delete(void* ptr) { /* deallocation logic */ }

flashcards.front: What is Placement New in C++?
flashcards.back: Placement new constructs an object at a specific memory location that's already allocated, using the syntax: new (address) ClassName(args);

flashcards.front: Why would you overload new and delete?
flashcards.back: To customize memory management for: 1) Better performance, 2) Memory leak detection, 3) Debugging, 4) Memory pooling, 5) Embedded systems with fixed memory.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between overloading new/delete globally vs per class?
flashcards.back: Global overload affects all allocations; class-specific overload affects only that class's allocations and takes precedence over global overload.

flashcards.front: What is the signature for class-specific new operator?
flashcards.back: void* operator new(size_t size) { return ::operator new(size); }

flashcards.front: What is new handler in C++?
flashcards.back: A function called when operator new fails to allocate memory. Set via std::set_new_handler() to handle allocation failures.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between new and new[]?
flashcards.back: new allocates memory for a single object and calls constructor; new[] allocates memory for an array and calls destructors for all elements.
Resource Acquisition Is Initialization - a technique where resources are acquired in constructor and released in destructor, ensuring automatic cleanup.

flashcards.front: What is the noexcept specification for overloaded new?
flashcards.back: By default, operator new is noexcept in C++14 and later, meaning it won't throw exceptions on failure (returns nullptr instead).

flashcards.front: How does delete[] differ from delete?
flashcards.back: delete[] calls destructor for each element in array then deallocates; delete calls single destructor then deallocates. Using wrong one causes undefined behavior.
Memory that was allocated but not freed before the program ends, causing the program to lose reference to allocated memory and waste resources.
A pre-allocated block of memory from which smaller allocations are served quickly, reducing fragmentation and allocation overhead.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between malloc/free and new/delete?
flashcards.back: malloc/free are C functions that don't call constructors/destructors; new/delete are C++ operators that handle object lifecycle properly.
